title: The Elder Scrolls III: Morrowind
notes: Played thousands of hours starting around age 7, before I could read fluently. Completed the game through spatial memory, pattern recognition, and world-reading rather than text. All-time favorite. The game that established how I process complex systems — by inhabiting them

title: The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion
notes: 600+ hours, most of it modded. One of the first games I could mod and run on my own machine. The vanilla systems are frustrating, but that was almost the point; it functioned more as a sandbox for understanding how games work than as a game I played straight. I experimented with small tweaks of my own and tried a ton of other people's mods. It opened my eyes to what was possible.

title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim
notes: 470 hours total across both editions. One of the defining games. Deep RPG investment, world-immersion.

title: The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt
notes: Quality-wise this should be a 9 or 10. The world feels more alive and lived-in than any game I've played since. Every quest feels real and important, and the player isn't the center of the universe, just another inhabitant of the world. Great build variety, phenomenal story, and the graphics pushed me to build a new PC which is another favorite hobby. The reason it's not higher is my own inability to commit to long story games as I've gotten older. I used to fully immerse in multi-dozen-hour campaigns. Now I play 10-20 hours, get busy, come back feeling lost, and end up starting over instead of pushing through. I've completed it once and played 30-40 hours into separate saves multiple times. I've experienced most of what it has to offer. The rating reflects me, not the game.

title: Fallout 4
notes: My least favorite Fallout game. I spent hundreds of hours on 3 and New Vegas on PS3 and was very excited for this one. Got it at release, spent a ton of time exploring the wasteland and even more time building bases. But the story is weak and the roleplaying elements are shallow. I haven't even completed the main story despite many attempts. It's a good action RPG but not a great Fallout game.

title: DARK SOULS III
notes: The first Dark Souls that actually clicked. I had tried DS1 earlier and bounced off it because it was so different from the RPGs I knew. DS3 got popular and something about it landed. Played it for a while, didn't finish, went back to DS1 and fell in love with the series there. Came back to DS3 with a fresh save and completed it. Built multiple characters for PvP, which pulled me into collecting nearly all items and endings in the process. DS1 is the favorite, but DS3 is the one that caught the deepest hyperfixation. Played it extensively for months and really haven't been back since.

title: ELDEN RING
meta: 164.6 hrs · Action RPG / Soulslike / Open World · 2022
notes: The most refined version of the Souls formula. Does everything I love about the DS games; character building, world building, and both the levels and bosses are top notch. The only reason I don't play it more is the same thing that makes it great. It's so vast that if I come back after time away I feel lost and disconnected. Same friction I have with long games generally, just applied to the best version of my favorite genre.

title: DARK SOULS: REMASTERED
notes: 242 hours combined across PTDE and Remastered. One of my all-time favorites. The game that defined the Soulslike for me — failure as a learning mechanism, mastery through repetition, a world that trusts you to figure it out.

title: DARK SOULS: Prepare To Die Edition
notes: Same game, revisited. 129 more hours on top of the 113 in the original. The investment across both editions puts this at the same level as DS3 and Skyrim

title: DARK SOULS II: Scholar of the First Sin
The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time
notes: Completed hundreds of times before I could read. Same pre-literacy pattern as Morrowind and Majora's Mask — navigating one of the most spatially complex games of its era through memory and pattern recognition alone.

title: The Legend of Zelda: Majora's Mask
notes: Completed multiple times before i could read. All-time favorite alongside Morrowind. The time-loop structure, the masks, the atmosphere of grief and impermanence. I have masks and concept art from the game guide in my home. The aesthetic has never left me.

title: Divinity: Original Sin 2
notes: Turn-based CRPG with genuine systems depth — one of the higher-investment titles outside the Soulslike catalog.

title: Doom II
notes: Played and modded since around age 12. Making maps in Doom 2 was my first real creative outlet. Not just playing — building. The origin of the maker instinct that shows up everywhere else in my life.

title: Far Cry 3
notes: Played extensively on PS3 as well. I loved the setting and the feeling of freedom and power progression. The simple loop of explore, fight, grow, done well. There was always something to do without feeling like the game was actively trying to bombard me with content.

title: Borderlands 2
notes: Most recent active play. Played on Steam Deck in small focused sessions — each one targeted at 1-3 objectives to strengthen my Commando build. Survival tree, Bee Shield, full legendary loadout. Level 52, completed TVHM. Used the save editor to level my legendaries — I know what I value about the experience and refused to waste limited time on the parts that don't deliver it.

title: Call of Duty: Black Ops II - Multiplayer
Call of Duty: Black Ops III
Call of Duty: Black Ops II - Zombies
Call of Duty: Black Ops II
Deus Ex: Human Revolution - Director's Cut
Vampire Survivors
notes: Same roguelite hook as Hades and Isaac — short sessions, build engineering, watching a system execute.

title: Hades
notes: Short runs, build optimization, high variance. The roguelite loop that fits ADHD reward structure.

title: Skater XL
notes: Skateboarding has been one of my great loves. I love the challenge, the process, and the artistic nature of seeing an everyday spot through the lens of how it can be experienced on a skateboard. This game is the purest simulation of that. It's physics-based and you genuinely can't play it without a fundamental understanding of how to manipulate the physics of a skateboard, gravity, and yourself. No score system, no goals, just a skateboarding simulation. There was a time in my life where I skated 4-6 hours every day. My body could only take so much and I was limited by daylight. Skater XL let me continue developing my skate brain when I physically couldn't be out there.

title: Sid Meier's Civilization V
notes: My first Civ game. I like building things that get stronger over time and the strategy makes me feel smart. The systems feel homey to me and I can always pick it up. I can't always put it down though, which is why I don't play it much anymore. I fall very easily into the one-more-turn trap

title: Cities: Skylines
notes: Pure builder. Same instinct as Minecraft and Doom 2 mapping — constructing and optimizing a system.

title: Minecraft
notes: Hundreds of hours in both Creative and Survival. Creative mode as digital architecture, Survival as systems management. Builder identity in its purest form

title: Stardew Valley
notes: The other half of the build loop I love. Small sessions, incremental progress, building something that compounds over time.

title: Grand Theft Auto V
notes: Pre-ordered on PS3 and played story mode extensively. For a long time it was my go-to turn-off-my-brain game; log on, drive around, run around an open world. Never got into the online mode and it turned me off that the game became all about it. Stopped playing for years, came back later and spent a good amount of time with single player mods, which is where most of these hours come from. Also used it as a benchmark for the PCs I built.

title: NARUTO SHIPPUDEN: Ultimate Ninja STORM Revolution
Injustice: Gods Among Us Ultimate Edition
